message:
fix(storage/git-lock): delete index.lock.owner before index.lock in
stale-lock reclaim so a racing git can't grab index.lock against a
stale owner metadata file

`try_clean_stale_git_lock` had four branches that all removed
`index.lock` *first* and then `index.lock.owner`. The unlink order
matters here: a concurrent git process that is in its own stale-lock
scanner sees `index.lock` as the signal that the guarded region is
free. If we remove `index.lock` before removing its companion
`index.lock.owner`, there is a small window where another git can
acquire a fresh `index.lock` and then immediately encounter our
dangling `index.lock.owner` from the old owner — which at minimum
confuses our own subsequent owner-PID heuristics (the alive/start-ticks
check below) and at worst makes a peer guard think the brand-new lock
is already stale and reclaim it again.

Flip the order in all four reclaim branches so `index.lock.owner` is
removed first and `index.lock` is removed last. That way any
concurrent scanner that sees `index.lock` missing also sees no stray
owner file, and any scanner that sees `index.lock` present can trust
the matching `.owner` because both files are either both present
(current owner) or both absent (fully reclaimed) — never "lock gone
but owner still here" which is the ambiguous state that triggered
the flaky behaviour. No logic change; just the unlink order in the
four branches covering:

1. owner PID reused (start-ticks mismatch)
2. owner PID alive but start-ticks unavailable, force-clean after
   2× max age
3. legacy owner-file format, alive PID, stale age
4. fallback age-only reclaim for orphaned lock (no owner file)

Matches the invariant the surrounding tracing logs already imply.
